core_intuitions: Raw crawl -> clean corpus is a cascade funnel: each stage strips a characteristic kind of junk (foreign-language, boilerplate/spam, exact mirror copies, near-duplicates, benchmark leakage) and only ~30% of raw tokens survive as high-signal training data.

MinHash: the minimum hash value over a set is equally likely to land on any element of the union, so two documents share a MinHash entry with probability EXACTLY equal to their Jaccard similarity |A intersect B| / |A union B| -- that is why a fraction of matching signature entries is an unbiased Jaccard estimator.

LSH banding turns the smooth MinHash estimator into a sharp yes/no threshold: split the k-row signature into b bands of r rows, call two docs candidates if ANY band matches exactly, and the resulting probability 1-(1-s^r)^b is an S-curve whose knee sits near (1/b)^(1/r) -- (b,r) is the knob trading recall against verification cost.
Exact dedup catches only byte-identical (normalized) copies; fuzzy dedup is needed for the far larger pool of near-duplicates (re-rendered HTML, mirror sites, shared boilerplate).

A Bloom filter replaces the exact-dedup hash SET with a fixed bit array + k hashes, with a deliberate asymmetry: no false negatives (a true duplicate is never emitted twice) but a tunable false-positive rate (rarely drops a unique doc) -- exactly the trade the dedup task wants.
Deduplication is not just storage: it cuts verbatim memorization, improves calibration, and raises the EFFECTIVE unique-token budget that scaling laws care about.

existing.name: dedup-paragraph-minhash-pipeline
existing.issue: Two rendering defects and a missed intuition. (1) The animated 'data token' squares are clipped at the very top of the viewBox in both themes -- they poke above the frame and read as stray fragments. (2) In dark mode the muted subtitle '>= 3 sentences or >= 100 chars' under 'Split into paragraphs' is nearly illegible (too-dark grey on the dark fill); other subtitles survive but this one is the worst. (3) It is a plain top-to-bottom flowchart; it conveys the STEP ORDER but not the one insight this section actually adds -- that the SAME boilerplate paragraph recurs across otherwise-unique documents, which is why dedup must drop to the paragraph level.
existing.improvement: Give the animated token a top margin (or start it inside the first box) so nothing is clipped; enlarge the viewBox top padding. Raise the dark-theme subtitle color to the same readable muted token used for body text so all subtitles are legible in both themes. Optionally strengthen the payload: near the 'Split into paragraphs' / 'Remove duplicate paragraphs' steps, show three DIFFERENT document cards that each contain one identical shaded 'About Us' paragraph, and after removal show that shared paragraph collapsed to a single kept copy while the unique paragraphs of each doc are retained -- this is the cross-document paragraph-recurrence intuition the prose describes.

new_figures.name: data-cleaning-cascade-funnel
new_figures.concept: The full raw-crawl-to-clean-corpus pipeline as a narrowing funnel, each stage labeled with the kind of junk it removes and the surviving token fraction.
new_figures.rationale: This is the spine of the entire chapter: the thesis is that a cascade of cheap-to-expensive filters converts chaotic crawl into a high-signal corpus, keeping only ~30% of raw tokens. A single funnel makes the whole chapter's structure and the compounding yield click at once, and anchors every individual technique (LangID, heuristics, exact/fuzzy dedup, decontamination) to its place and its cost in the cascade.
new_figures.placement: Putting It All Together: A Production Pipeline
new_figures.brief: A vertical funnel (widest at top, narrowing downward) representing the token stream shrinking through pipeline stages. Each stage is a horizontal band whose WIDTH is proportional to the surviving token fraction, stacked top to bottom: (1) Raw WET crawl -- full width, label 'raw crawl (100%)'; (2) Language ID -- narrower, removed slice labeled 'foreign-language + low-confidence docs', survivor '~60%'; (3) Heuristic quality filters -- 'boilerplate, spam, encoding garbage', '~45%'; (4) Exact dedup (SHA-256 / Bloom) -- 'byte-identical mirror & syndicated copies', '~40%'; (5) Fuzzy dedup (MinHash + LSH) -- 'near-duplicates: re-rendered HTML, shared boilerplate', '~30%'; (6) Benchmark decontamination -- 'leaked eval questions', '~29.9% (approx 30%)'. On the RIGHT of each band, show a small 'removed' chip depicting the discarded slice, sized to the drop. At the bottom, a bold callout: 'final yield ~30% of raw tokens -- higher diversity, lower memorization, honest evals'. Use a neutral theme-safe stage color for the surviving funnel and a muted/red-toned chip for removed material; do NOT hardcode brand primaries. IMPORTANT: annotate somewhere small 'illustrative proportions from the chapter worked example, not measured benchmarks'. Percentages are the chapter's own worked-example figures; invent no new numbers. Everything must fit inside the viewBox with a clear top-to-bottom reading order; the narrowing widths carry the intuition even before the labels are read.

new_figures.name: minhash-jaccard-estimator
new_figures.concept: Why the min of a random hash over a shingle set collides between two documents with probability exactly equal to their Jaccard similarity.
new_figures.rationale: MinHash is the single most elegant and most easily mis-understood mechanism in the chapter. The prose states P[m_j(A)=m_j(B)] = J(A,B) as a fact; the WHY (the minimum over the union is equally likely to be any element, and it matches iff that argmin lands in the intersection) is exactly the intuition a static figure can deliver and text cannot. Grasping this makes the whole signature-fraction-estimates-Jaccard idea obvious rather than magical.
new_figures.brief: Two documents A and B drawn as shingle sets (small chips, e.g. 5-6 shingles each) with a visible overlap region (the intersection) -- draw them as a Venn-style union: elements only-in-A, shared (intersection), only-in-B. Below/beside, show ONE random hash function h relabeling every shingle in the UNION with a hash value, then sort the union by hash value into a single row. Circle the SMALLEST hash value (the global minimum of the union). Make the visual point in two panels: (left) when that global-minimum element lies in the INTERSECTION, min h(A) == min h(B) -> the signatures MATCH; (right) when the global minimum lies in an only-A or only-B region, the mins DIFFER -> no match. Add the punchline equation P[match] = |A intersect B| / |A union B| = J(A,B), with a one-line gloss 'the min is equally likely to be any union element; it matches exactly when it falls in the shared part'. Then a small strip showing k independent hashes give k independent coin flips, so fraction-of-matching-entries estimates J with error ~ 1/sqrt(k). Use small illustrative integer hash values; theme-safe colors, intersection shaded distinctly from the two exclusive regions in both light and dark. No overlaps, clear left-to-right then top-to-bottom reading order.

new_figures.name: lsh-banding-scurve
new_figures.concept: How splitting a MinHash signature into b bands of r rows converts the linear match probability into a sharp S-curve with a tunable near-duplicate threshold.
new_figures.rationale: LSH is what makes dedup scale from O(N^2) to near-linear, and the (b,r) banding is the knob every practitioner must set. The key intuition -- 'candidate if ANY band matches on ALL r rows' produces an S-shaped acceptance curve whose knee is (1/b)^(1/r) -- is geometric and belongs in a figure. It directly supports the worked example (b=16,r=8 -> knee ~0.71) and the recall-vs-verification-cost trade the chapter emphasizes.
new_figures.brief: Left half: a single tall signature column of k rows (use a small illustrative k, e.g. 12 or 16 cells) bracketed into b bands of r rows each (e.g. 4 bands of 4, or 8 bands of 2 -- pick small values and label them b and r). Show two adjacent signature columns for docs X and Y; highlight ONE band where all r cells are identical between X and Y, with a callout 'match on all r rows in >=1 band -> candidate pair'. Annotate that the docs go into the same hash bucket for that band. Right half: a plot of P(candidate | s) = 1 - (1 - s^r)^b on axes s (Jaccard, 0..1) vs probability (0..1). Draw the characteristic S-curve, mark the knee/threshold t* ~ (1/b)^(1/r) with a dashed vertical line, and shade the region above t* 'almost always caught (recall)' and the low-s region 'almost always skipped (little wasted verification)'. Optionally overlay two faint alternative curves for a different (b,r) split to show the knob moving the threshold left/right and sharpening the step (e.g. more/longer bands). Add a one-line note: 'set the knee just BELOW your true target, then verify candidates exactly'. Theme-safe colors, the S-curve and threshold line legible in both themes; small illustrative integers only; everything inside the viewBox with the signature-mechanism (left) reading into the resulting curve (right).

new_figures.name: bloom-filter-dedup-asymmetry
new_figures.concept: A Bloom filter as a bit array + k hashes replacing the exact-dedup hash set, with no false negatives but a tunable false-positive rate.
new_figures.rationale: The Bloom section carries a genuine and easy-to-invert insight: the error is one-sided, and the direction of the error is exactly the one dedup can tolerate (a false positive drops one unique doc in ~10^6; a true duplicate is NEVER kept). A static figure of insert/query on the shared bit array makes both the mechanism and the asymmetry concrete far faster than the prose + formulas. Priority 3 because the prose+math already cover it well; the figure is a strong reinforcement rather than the chapter's central mechanism.
new_figures.brief: Center: one horizontal bit array of m cells (use a small illustrative m, e.g. 16-20 bits, most 0). Top: an INSERT operation -- take item 'doc-A', draw k=3 arrows (illustrative k) from it to k cells, flipping those cells to 1 ('set the k bits it hashes to'). Middle: a QUERY of a TRUE DUPLICATE 'doc-A again' -- its k arrows land on already-set 1 bits -> 'all bits set -> reported SEEN -> correctly skipped'. Bottom: two contrasting queries side by side to nail the asymmetry: (a) a genuinely NEW doc whose k bits are not all set -> 'at least one 0 -> definitely NEW -> kept' (this is why there are NO false negatives / a real duplicate can never slip through); (b) a rare NEW doc that happens to collide on all k already-set bits -> 'false positive -> a unique doc wrongly dropped (rate ~p)'. Add a compact caption box: 'no false negatives (a true duplicate is never kept twice) + tunable false-positive rate -- fits dedup: you can bound the unique-doc loss.' Include a small space-saving footnote 'bit array, not a growing hash set -> fixed memory'. Theme-safe colors: set bits visually distinct from unset in both themes, the false-positive path marked in a caution tone. Small illustrative integers only (do not restate the 3.6 GB / 28.8 bits figures as asserted results; they are derivations in the prose). All inside the viewBox, clear top-to-bottom reading.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich but figure-poor: a single narrow flowchart covers only the paragraph-level dedup slice, while the chapter's three load-bearing intuitions -- the cleaning cascade funnel, the MinHash-estimates-Jaccard lemma, and the LSH banding S-curve -- have no visual. Refine the existing figure (fix top clipping of the animated token and the illegible dark-mode subtitle; optionally add the cross-document shared-paragraph payload). Add three foundational statics -- the cascade funnel (chapter spine), the MinHash Jaccard-estimator (the elegant why), and the LSH banding S-curve (the scaling knob) -- plus a priority-3 Bloom-filter asymmetry figure. All static: none of these mechanisms requires motion to convey.
